question.text: An investment adviser is evaluating tax-saving options for Neha, a 30-year-old salaried client who has opted for the new tax regime for FY 2025-26. Which of the following strategies would provide her a valid tax benefit?

answer.text: Invest Rs.1,50,000 in ELSS funds to claim deduction under Section 80C
Purchase health insurance of Rs.25,000 to claim deduction under Section 80D
Request employer to increase NPS contribution within the 14% salary limit to claim deduction under Section 80CCD(2)
Make additional NPS contribution of Rs.50,000 to claim deduction under Section 80CCD(1B)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under the new tax regime, Sections 80C, 80D, and 80CCD(1B) are NOT available. However, Section 80CCD(2) — employer's contribution to NPS — is available in both old and new regimes. For private sector employees, the limit is 14% of salary under the new regime. This is the only valid tax-saving strategy among the options for someone on the new regime. **The Trap:** Options A, B, and D are all legitimate tax-saving strategies under the OLD regime but are explicitly excluded from the new regime. Many candidates assume all NPS deductions are treated the same — but 80CCD(1B) (self-contribution) is NOT available in the new regime while 80CCD(2) (employer contribution) IS. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-B directly tests advisers' ability to recommend appropriate strategies based on the chosen tax regime. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For new regime clients, the ONLY Chapter VI-A deduction levers are 80CCD(2) and 80CCH. Focus advisory conversations on salary restructuring to maximize employer NPS contribution rather than pushing traditional 80C instruments.
